Proxy Calls Guide
When and how to ask smart questions to team leads and PMs

🎯 The Three Mindset Rules

1. Purpose Over People — Lawyer on email ≠ automatic privilege. Ask: What is the PURPOSE of this communication?
2. When in Doubt — Protect & Escalate — 50/50, 60/40, or unsure? Code as Privileged and escalate immediately.
3. Four Corners & Discipline — Decide only from what is written in the document itself.

📊 QC Score Gates

ScoreBandPrivilege Live Coding
100%◆ Gold Standard ◆✅ Eligible — QC Candidate
96–98%⭐ Excellent❌ Training only
90–94%✅ Solid❌ Targeted coaching
80–88%⚠️ Developing❌ High QC required
<80%🔴 Retraining❌ Intensive retraining

Attorney–Client Privilege (ACP)

Protects confidential communications between an attorney and client for the purpose of seeking or providing legal advice.

Four Elements (ALL must be true):

  1. Communication — written, oral, or electronic
  2. Between attorney & client (or their agents)
  3. Made in confidence
  4. Primary purpose: legal advice — not just business

✅ Usually ACP:

  • Employee asking counsel: "What is our legal exposure if we do X?"
  • Outside counsel memo analyzing litigation risk to small internal group
  • CEO to outside counsel: "Here are the facts; what are our legal options?"

❌ Usually NOT ACP:

  • Business emails with lawyer copied only as "FYI"
  • Marketing presentations reviewed by legal but containing no legal advice
  • In-house counsel acting as Head of Strategy with no legal content

Work Product Privilege (WPP)

Protects materials prepared in anticipation of litigation or for trial by or for a party or its representatives.

Key Test: "Would this document exist in this form if there were no litigation or investigation?" If NO → likely WPP.

Key Points:

  • Does NOT require an attorney on the document
  • Covers: notes, drafts, chronologies, investigation summaries, strategy docs
  • "Legal asked us to compile this for the DOJ investigation" → WPP signal

Common Interest Privilege (CIP) / Joint Defense

Allows parties with a shared legal interest to share privileged info without waiving attorney-client privilege.

CIP Checklist:

  • Parties share a legal interest (not just commercial)
  • Lawyers are involved in the communication
  • Communication made to advance that legal interest
  • No active waiver — information kept confidential
If you see: "common interest," "joint defense," "JDA" → treat as Privileged and escalate if unclear.

In-House vs Outside Counsel

Core Rule: Privilege follows ROLE, not job title.

Lawyer Hat → ACP:

  • Advising on antitrust risk of a pricing structure
  • Legal analysis of employment discrimination exposure
  • Recommending actions to reduce legal exposure

Business Hat → NOT Privileged:

  • "Our Q4 numbers are weak — let's cut headcount" (no legal content)
  • Meeting invite to weekly sales check-in
  • Holiday party planning with counsel accidentally copied

Regulators & Bank Examination Privilege

AgencyFocus
SECSecurities markets, investor protection
DOJFederal law enforcement, antitrust
FTCConsumer protection, antitrust
FERCInterstate energy transmission
IRSFederal tax administration
OSHAWorkplace safety
FDADrug, device, food safety
FBIFederal criminal investigations
CMSMedicare & Medicaid
Bank Exam Privilege: Always code as Privileged and Escalate. Never strip protection.

Four Corners Test

Only use what is written inside the four corners of the document itself.

Do NOT code Responsive just because:

  • The custodian is "usually" responsive
  • The date is within a responsive range
  • The parent or sibling document is responsive

Drafts, Families & Attachments

Drafts & Privilege:

  • Drafts can be privileged — especially with legal comments by counsel
  • Privilege weaker when attorney is doing business/accounting work
  • Drafts intended for public/government filing may lose privilege

Families (Parent + Attachments):

  • If overall family context is privileged → safeguard the whole family unless protocol says otherwise
  • A clearly privileged attachment remains privileged even with a neutral parent

Waiver of Privilege

Privilege is LOST when: client discloses to a non-agent third party · attorney discloses to a third party · email sent with outside third party as recipient · final document filed publicly.
Coder's rule: Do NOT decide waiver yourself. Mark Privileged and Escalate.

Coding Rules & Conservative Approach

SituationAction
50/50 privilege vs notPrivileged + Escalate
60/40 or 40/60Privileged + Escalate
Gray area / unsurePrivileged + Escalate
Bank exam / supervisory reportPrivileged + Escalate
Clear ACP onlyACP
Clear WPP onlyWPP
Both applyACP + WPP
CIPCIP
Clearly not privilegedNot Privileged

🔄 Advanced Review Workflows

✏️ Redaction Workflow — Triggers automatically when you code a document as Redact in the production action field. Two methods:
① Modal Redaction: Full-screen workflow with reason codes (Privilege, PII, Privacy, Relevance), location field, and reviewer note. Add multiple marks per document.
② Drag-to-Redact: Switch to Redaction Mode using the dropdown above the coding panel. Select any text in the document viewer — a popup appears to confirm the reason code and note before applying the mark. Marks appear inline as [REDACTED: Privilege] chips.
📋 Privilege Log (PLOG) Workflow — Triggers automatically after any Withhold or Redact coding where privilege applies. Two methods:
① Auto PLOG Modal: Opens after Save Coding with all metadata pre-filled (Doc ID, From, To, Date, Subject, Custodian, Type). Select ACP / WPP / ACP+WPP / CIP, write your description, and the real-time quality checker flags weak entries.
② Inline PLOG Mode: Switch to PLOG Mode using the dropdown above the coding panel. Fill the quick PLOG form directly in the panel without leaving the document. Quality checker runs live.
Quality Checker Rules: Your PLOG description must be 60+ characters · include privilege indicators (attorney, legal advice, work product, anticipation of litigation) · not reveal the privileged content itself · pass all three checks before saving.
WARNING: Mis-coding a privileged doc as Not Privileged = opposing counsel sees ALL privileged content.

🔺 Escalation Chain

When in doubt, follow this exact order — never guess silently on a gray area.

1
Glory or Junior
Your first go-to for ALL questions
↓
2
Zabron or Simon
If Glory/Junior are unavailable or need backup
↓
3
Aaron
If still unclear after steps 1–2
↓
4
PM / RM
If Aaron doesn't have a clear answer or it's client-level
While waiting → keep coded as Privileged. You are never penalized for protecting privilege and asking for help.

📝 How to Ask a Good Question

  1. Doc ID — exact identifier
  2. Short context — who is emailing whom, about what
  3. Your lean — what you think and why
  4. Why you're unsure — what makes it difficult
"Doc ID: ABC-12345
Sender: in-house counsel; recipients: two managers
Content: Counsel asks managers for facts to assess FTC investigation risk.
I'm leaning: Responsive, ACP + WPP, Highly Confidential.
Question: Is this the right privilege/confidentiality combo for regulator-related internal emails?"

⏱️ Professionalism Reminders

  • Respond to messages within 5 minutes while on shift
  • Report first document coded at start of shift
  • By 1:00 pm NY time — report total documents coded
  • Target speed: Day 1 ~20/hr → Day 2 ~30/hr → Day 3 ~40/hr → Day 4+ 50–60/hr
  • Never sacrifice privilege accuracy for speed

⚫ Privilege Withhold vs. Privilege Redact

🚫

Privilege WITHHOLD

Hide the ENTIRE document. The opposing side gets nothing but a blank placeholder (slipsheet).

When: The entire purpose is legal advice or lawsuit strategy.
Example: CEO to lawyer: "Can we legally fire John?" → WITHHOLD
✏️

Privilege REDACT

Hide only a PART of the document. Black box over the secret sentences, give the rest to opposing side.

When: Mostly business, small piece of legal advice mixed in.
Example: 10-page Minutes. Pages 1–9 business, page 10 has one legal paragraph → REDACT
Simple Rule: If the WHOLE document is a secret → WITHHOLD. If only a SMALL PIECE is a secret → REDACT.

📊 Side by Side

🚫 WITHHOLD✏️ REDACT
What happensEntire document hidden; replaced with a slipsheetOnly the privileged portion is blacked out; rest is produced
Opposing side seesNothing — just a blank placeholderThe non-privileged portions
PLOG entry requiredYesYes

📋 The Privilege Log (PLOG) Guide

The Golden Rule: Write a description that proves a lawyer was involved, but NEVER give away the actual secret.

✅ GOOD PLOG Examples:

✅ "Email reflecting legal advice regarding contract validity."
✅ "Memorandum prepared in anticipation of litigation regarding patent claims."

❌ BAD PLOG Examples:

❌ "Email from lawyer saying we will lose the case because our contract is illegal." → Gives away the secret!
❌ "Email." → Too vague. The judge will reject this.

📖 eDiscovery Playbook

Training handbook for reviewers, supervisors, and QC — Version 1.1 | March 31, 2026

⚠️ Important: Project-specific instructions always control. If a case team, client, or agency gives a different rule, follow that rule first and use this playbook as the baseline.

📌 Core Review Standards

TopicRule
AccuracyDo not guess. Review the document, think, and code what the document actually says.
ConsistencyUse the same rule the same way every time.
EscalationAsk early when a document is unclear, sensitive, or risky.
ProfessionalismWrite clear notes, follow instructions, and keep client work confidential.

📊 Pace vs Accuracy — Universal Principles

Speed matters, but only after accuracy is stable. A good reviewer is steady, not rushed.
  • If you start missing obvious things, slow down immediately
  • Long shifts cause mistakes — take short breaks and reset your focus
  • Never sacrifice accuracy just to hit a number
  • A fast wrong review is worse than a slower correct review
🏛️ P

Strict and Defensible

Precision matters more than aggressive speed. Best fit: reviewers who can follow rules closely and write clean notes.

Agency Priorities
Exact rule-followingClean escalationLow error rateDefensible coding
Unique Rules
  • Do not assume documents are non-responsive just because they look routine
  • Privilege issues should be escalated early
  • Double-check emails with legal counsel names or law firm domains
Red Flags
Legal counsel names in the header, body, or signature block
Attachments that change the meaning of the main email
Mixed personal and business content in the same thread
Language that sounds like advice, direction, instruction, or complaint

⚡ Pace: 35–50 docs/hour

🧠 Mental Strategies

  • Read subject line, sender, recipient, and date first
  • Look at the whole thread before coding the last message only
  • Check attachments before finalizing a decision
  • Say the decision to yourself in plain English before clicking

🔺 Escalate When

✓
Privilege is unclear
✓
Document could be hot, sensitive, or high risk
✓
Instructions seem to conflict with the document
✓
Reviewer cannot confidently explain the coding decision
Keep escalation messages short: Doc ID · Issue (1 sentence) · Your best call · Why you're unsure

🖥️ Review Platform Training

  • Open a document from the queue, batch, or saved search
  • Use the document viewer to read text, email thread, and attachments
  • Apply coding in the coding pane or layout fields
  • Use highlight tools to find names, dates, privilege clues, and key terms
  • ❌ Common mistake: coding before reading attachments or context
  • 💡 Learn keyboard shortcuts — but never let shortcuts replace judgment
  • Open the queue or worklist assigned to you
  • Review document image, text, and metadata together
  • Apply tags and statuses in the side panel or coding form
  • ❌ Common mistake: moving too fast without checking the thread
  • Open the review set or document list assigned to your project
  • Read the document, thread, and related family items together
  • Apply tags, fields, or issue codes in the review panel
  • ❌ Common mistake: forgetting to review attachments and family members

✅ TOP 10 DO's

  • Read the whole document
  • Check attachments and thread context
  • Follow project instructions exactly
  • Ask when a document is unclear
  • Keep notes short and factual
  • Stay consistent with similar documents
  • Work at a sustainable pace
  • Keep client info confidential
  • Double-check privilege issues
  • Communicate early if stuck

❌ TOP 10 DON'Ts

  • Do not guess
  • Do not ignore attachments
  • Do not rush edge cases
  • Do not change standard when tired
  • Do not leave unclear docs unflagged
  • Do not write vague escalation notes
  • Do not code by habit only
  • Do not treat every project the same
  • Do not hide mistakes
  • Do not produce work you can't explain
Read carefully · Code consistently · Escalate early · Never guess
⚖️ A

Balanced, Analytical, Discussion-Driven

Attorney-heavy workflow. Pay attention to legal advice hidden in business language. Best fit: reviewers who can think clearly and explain their decisions.

Agency Priorities
Context analysisPrivilege spottingReasoned escalationConsistent issue coding
Unique Rules
  • Look for legal advice hidden inside business language
  • Do not ignore attachment chains
  • Notes should explain the logic behind a call
Red Flags
Legal counsel names in the header, body, or signature block
Attachments that change the meaning of the main email
Mixed personal and business content in the same thread
Language that sounds like advice, direction, instruction, or complaint
Common Reviewer Mistakes
  • Only reading the first page or first message
  • Missing hidden attachments
  • Coding too quickly without context

⚡ Pace: 40–55 docs/hour

  • Open a document from the queue, batch, or saved search
  • Use the document viewer to read text, email thread, and attachments
  • Apply coding in the coding pane or layout fields
  • ❌ Common mistake: coding before reading attachments or context
  • Open the queue or worklist assigned to you
  • Review document image, text, and metadata together
  • ❌ Common mistake: moving too fast without checking the thread
  • Open the review set or document list assigned to your project
  • Read the document, thread, and related family items together
  • ❌ Common mistake: forgetting to review attachments and family members

✅ TOP 10 DO's

  • Read the whole document
  • Check attachments and thread context
  • Follow project instructions exactly
  • Ask when a document is unclear
  • Keep notes short and factual
  • Stay consistent with similar documents
  • Work at a sustainable pace
  • Keep client info confidential
  • Double-check privilege issues
  • Communicate early if stuck

❌ TOP 10 DON'Ts

  • Do not guess
  • Do not ignore attachments
  • Do not rush edge cases
  • Do not change standard when tired
  • Do not leave unclear docs unflagged
  • Do not write vague escalation notes
  • Do not code by habit only
  • Do not treat every project the same
  • Do not hide mistakes
  • Do not produce work you can't explain
📋 C — Complete Discovery Source

Structured and Workflow-Heavy

Highly process-oriented. Focus on consistency, documentation, and reliable production flow. Best fit: organized reviewers who are careful with platform steps.

Agency Priorities
Batch disciplineConsistencyClean taggingLow rework
Unique Rules
  • Follow folder, batch, and queue rules exactly
  • Keep an eye on saved searches and document sets
  • Do not drift from the batch standard because a document looks unusual
Common Mistakes
  • Jumping ahead in the queue
  • Using inconsistent coding across similar documents
  • Ignoring batch instructions

⚡ Pace: steady and sustainable (accuracy first)

✅ TOP 10 DO's

  • Read the whole document
  • Check attachments and thread context
  • Follow project instructions exactly
  • Ask when a document is unclear
  • Keep notes short and factual
  • Stay consistent with similar documents
  • Work at a sustainable pace
  • Keep client info confidential
  • Double-check privilege issues
  • Communicate early if stuck

❌ TOP 10 DON'Ts

  • Do not guess
  • Do not ignore attachments
  • Do not rush edge cases
  • Do not change standard when tired
  • Do not leave unclear docs unflagged
  • Do not write vague escalation notes
  • Do not code by habit only
  • Do not treat every project the same
  • Do not hide mistakes
  • Do not produce work you can't explain
🚀 T

Operational, Metrics-Aware, Production-Oriented

Production-focused. Projects move quickly — balance pace with quality. Rewards clean throughput, strong discipline, and fast escalation of exceptions.

Agency Priorities
ThroughputClear communicationException handlingReliable accuracy
Unique Rules
  • Do not hide uncertainty
  • Use short and direct escalation notes
  • Watch deadlines and production checkpoints closely
Common Mistakes
  • Going silent on unclear documents
  • Missing deadlines because of avoidable rework
  • Treating speed as more important than defensibility

⚡ Pace: 40–50 docs/hour

✅ TOP 10 DO's

  • Read the whole document
  • Check attachments and thread context
  • Follow project instructions exactly
  • Ask when a document is unclear
  • Keep notes short and factual
  • Stay consistent with similar documents
  • Work at a sustainable pace
  • Keep client info confidential
  • Double-check privilege issues
  • Communicate early if stuck

❌ TOP 10 DON'Ts

  • Do not guess
  • Do not ignore attachments
  • Do not rush edge cases
  • Do not change standard when tired
  • Do not leave unclear docs unflagged
  • Do not write vague escalation notes
  • Do not code by habit only
  • Do not treat every project the same
  • Do not hide mistakes
  • Do not produce work you can't explain

📞 Proxy Calls Guide

When should reviewers ask questions to team leads or project managers? And how do you ask in a way that shows understanding, gets a fast useful answer, and builds your professional reputation?

🤔 When Should You Ask?

✅ ESCALATE THESE — Always Ask
🔴 Genuine 50/50 Privilege Gray Area
When: A document has a lawyer's name but the content is ambiguous — could be legal advice or a business decision.
Doc ID [X]. In-house counsel is on this email chain. The subject is "Q4 Strategy" and the content mixes business forecasts with what could be a legal risk analysis on page 3. I'm currently leaning ACP with a note to escalate, but I want to confirm whether the mixed-purpose nature here changes my call or whether I should withhold the entire chain.
Why this works: You've already formed a lean, identified the exact ambiguity, and you're asking a precise question — not asking someone else to do your thinking.
Team LeadPM
🔴 Protocol Conflict — Instructions Don't Match the Document
When: The project protocol says X, but the document is a clear Y. Proceeding either way feels wrong.
The protocol says to code emails to external parties as non-responsive, but this document is addressed to opposing counsel's assistant and contains what looks like a production acknowledgment letter. The protocol doesn't seem to anticipate this type of document. Before I code it non-responsive or escalate further, can you confirm the intent for this category?
Why this works: You spotted a gap in the instructions, described the exact conflict in one sentence, and you're asking for clarification — not a free decision.
Team Lead
🔴 Hot Document
When: You believe a document is substantively important — could directly affect the matter.
I want to flag Doc ID [X] as potentially significant. It's an internal email chain between two executives referencing a meeting with the regulator that isn't mentioned in the protocol's hot custodian list. The content discusses the specific timeline of events the case appears to center on. I've coded it responsive and escalated — just wanted to flag it verbally so you can decide whether it needs QC attention immediately.
Why this works: You've already acted (coded and escalated), you're providing context, not asking someone to review it for you.
Team LeadPM
⛔ DO NOT ESCALATE THESE — Handle Yourself
Does this email need to be coded? (Yes — every document needs a code)
This looks non-responsive, right? (If obvious, trust yourself)
I don't know what ACP means — can you explain? (Use the Reference Guide first)
Should I work faster? (Self-manage your pace)
There's a spelling error in this email — should I flag it? (No)

🧠 Smart Question Formula

Every good question to a team lead or PM has the same four parts.

1
Doc ID / Reference
Always start with the exact document identifier
↓
2
Context in 1–2 sentences
Who is talking to whom? What is the key fact?
↓
3
Your current lean / decision
Tell them what you think the answer is. Showing you've thought about it saves time.
↓
4
The specific question
One precise question they can answer in one sentence.
Never ask a question you could have answered by reading the protocol or this playbook. Every question should demonstrate that you've already done your work.

📋 Smart Questions by Scenario

📧 Email Thread Complexity
What do I do with this email chain?
Doc [X] is a 12-message email chain. The first 8 messages are pure business. Message 9 is from in-house counsel containing what I believe is legal advice. My coding instinct is to code the chain as ACP based on message 9, but I want to confirm — should I break the chain and code the first 8 separately, or keep the family together as privileged?
Team Lead
📎 Attachment Puzzle
Does the attachment follow the email?
Doc [X] is a routine marketing email with a spreadsheet attached. The spreadsheet has a tab labeled "Legal Risk Analysis" that appears to have been prepared by outside counsel. The parent email has no legal content. Protocol says family follows parent, but this attachment seems independently privileged. Should I apply family rule or flag the attachment separately?
Team Lead
⏱️ Pace / Production Check-In
I'm going slow. What should I do?
I want to flag a pace issue proactively. I'm currently averaging 28 docs/hour against a 40/hour target. About 60% of my batch are long email threads with 10+ messages and attachments. I've been reading each fully, which is slowing me. Should I adjust my approach for this batch type, or is the pace understood given the complexity?
Team Lead

💬 Communication Tone Guide

Situation❌ Avoid✅ Use Instead
Asking about a doc"This email is confusing, what is it?""Doc [X] has ambiguous privilege indicators — here's my lean and my specific question..."
Reporting a problem"I've been waiting on this for an hour""Flagging that Doc [X] is pending escalation — wanted to ensure it's in the queue"
Sharing a mistake"I think I might have done this wrong""I identified a possible error in Doc [X]. I coded it as [Y] but believe correct code is [Z]. Can you confirm before I adjust?"
Checking pace"I'm behind""Proactive check-in: I'm at [X] docs at [time]. My batch has been high-complexity — wanted to flag this early."

🚫 Red Flags — Questions That Signal a Training Gap

Repeatedly asking "Is this privileged?" without a stated lean — shows you aren't applying the four-step test
Asking about the same document type multiple times — signals you haven't internalized the rule
Asking questions directly answered in the protocol — signals you aren't reading project instructions thoroughly
Vague questions with no doc ID — wastes everyone's time and makes you appear disorganized
The best reviewers ask fewer questions — but each one they ask is sharp, precise, and demonstrates serious thinking.

🧪 Pharma · FDA Regulatory Breach
💊 Harmon v. NovaCure Pharmaceuticals
FDA Regulatory Breach & Whistleblower Retaliation · Full Redaction + PLOG Training Set
Issue 1: FDA Regulatory Breach Issue 2: Product Liability Issue 3: Corporate Fraud Issue 4: Whistleblower Retaliation

/ 510 coded

Open in Relativity →

📋 Case Background

Joba Company is a mid-sized technology firm specializing in proprietary data analytics software. Bukando was a Senior Data Engineer employed by Joba Company for four years. Upon resignation, Joba Company alleges Bukando copied proprietary source code, client data models, and pricing algorithms to a USB drive before his last day — materials covered under a signed NDA and employment agreement. Bukando subsequently joined Rival Analytics Inc., a direct competitor.

Bukando's position (via Kiwingo LLP): The termination was constructive dismissal. He disputes that the materials were trade secrets and claims all work he took was work he personally created and is entitled to retain. He has filed a wrongful termination counterclaim.

🎯 Review Instructions

Protocol Note: These project instructions govern. Apply the AA Team privilege standards and this project's issue definitions.

Issue Definitions

Issue 1: NDA Breach

Any document discussing, referencing, or evidencing the NDA signed by Bukando, violations thereof, or awareness of confidentiality obligations.

Issue 2: Trade Secret Theft

Any document relating to proprietary code, algorithms, data models, pricing structures, client lists, or evidence of unauthorized copying or transfer of Joba Company assets.

Issue 3: Competitive Damage

Any document showing Bukando's use of Joba's proprietary materials at Rival Analytics, or demonstrating competitive harm to Joba Company.

Issue 4: Wrongful Termination (Counter)

Any document relating to Bukando's employment conditions, constructive dismissal allegations, performance reviews, or HR communications.

Reviewer Note
💡 Best Practice Describe the location and reason precisely. Vague redactions can be challenged. The opposing side sees a black box — your note is for internal QC only.
📋
Privilege Log Entry

📋 Project Protocols

VeridianBank European Data Breach — Review Protocol

Platform: Everlaw. Coding fields differ from Relativity — familiarise yourself with the GDPR-specific issue tags before beginning.

Case Overview

VeridianBank PLC suffered a breach of 4.2 million customer records including biometric authentication templates, financial data, and health-linked transaction data. Three European regulators (ICO — UK, CNIL — France, BaFin — Germany) are investigating. A class action of up to 4.2 million data subjects is pending.

Issue Definitions

IssueDefinitionKey Facts
Issue 1 — Art.32Inadequate technical security measuresBiometric data in plaintext; vendor MFA gap; known pen test findings not remediated
Issue 2 — Art.33/34Late/inadequate breach notification72h clock started 01:14 AM; CEO instructed delay; notification at 112–125 hours; misleading Art.34 language
Issue 3 — Art.9Special category biometric data protection4.2M biometric templates; 341K health-linked records; mandatory higher protection standard
Issue 4 — WhistleblowerCISO retaliation (PIDA)CISO terminated 7 days after ICO submission; PIP initiated same week as breach; PIDA claim filed
GDPR Privilege Notes: Legal professional privilege applies under UK law (equivalent to ACP). GDPR Art. 38 requires DPO independence — communications to/from DPO may not be privileged. Regulator correspondence (ICO, CNIL, BaFin) is never privileged. Attorney advice on GDPR compliance strategy is ACP.
Special Category Alert: Any document containing biometric template data, health data, or genetic data is Art. 9 special category — apply heightened protection. Code as Issue 3 AND relevant other issues.

QuantumEdge AI Flash Crash — Review Protocol

Platform: Relativity. Standard coding fields apply. Note: AI/algorithmic trading technical documents require careful reading — focus on what was KNOWN vs. DISCLOSED.

Case Overview

QuantumEdge AI's APEX trading algorithm entered a positive feedback loop on March 15, 2024, executing 847,000 sell orders in 23 minutes and causing a $4.7B market cap decline. The SEC, CFTC, and DOJ are investigating securities fraud, market manipulation, insider trading, and failure to supervise. A class action by 14,847 investors claims $2.1B.

Issue Definitions

IssueDefinitionKey Facts
Issue 1 — Securities FraudFalse/misleading statements in investor materials re APEX safetyPitch decks claimed working circuit breakers; December 2023 report showed failure mode; kill switch latency falsely stated
Issue 2 — Market ManipulationAPEX behavior during crash constitutes manipulation847K sell orders, 40% of market volume; position limit override; feedback loop creating artificial prices
Issue 3 — Insider TradingCEO sold $6M + bought puts 3 days before crashReceived CRO risk warning March 8; sold March 12; crash March 15; put options returned $4.8M
Issue 4 — Failure to SuperviseInadequate algorithmic risk controlsKnown circuit breaker defect; undocumented position limit override; kill switch overwhelmed at live trading speed
Privilege Notes: Engineering reports and internal memos are NOT privileged (business documents). Attorney-client privilege requires legal advice from counsel. The forensic trading logs are not privileged — they are factual records. The Wells Submission and SEC response strategy memos are ACP + WPP.
Insider Trading Alert: Any document showing what the CEO knew before March 12 (the trade date) is critical for Issue 3. The sequence — March 8 (CRO warning) → March 8 (CEO dismisses) → March 12 (CEO sells) → March 15 (crash) — must be reconstructed from the documents.

📋 Protocol — CADE v. TechBrasil Consortium

Language: Documents are in Brazilian Portuguese. The protocol is in English. Use translation tools as needed for unfamiliar terminology. Key legal terms are glossed in the issue tag descriptions below.

Case Overview

The Brazilian competition authority (CADE — Conselho Administrativo de Defesa Econômica) opened Administrative Process No. 08700.004521/2022-31 against TechBrasil SA and three co-conspirators (Nexatel Ltda, DataComm Brasil, SulTech Sistemas) for a price-fixing cartel, market allocation scheme, bid-rigging on government contracts, and related violations of Lei 12.529/2011 (Brazil's antitrust law). TechBrasil SA is our client (defense). A leniency agreement negotiation is ongoing.

Key Parties

PartyRoleKey Custodians
TechBrasil SAOur Client (Defendant)CEO Ricardo Almeida, CFO Mariana Costa, GC Paulo Ferreira, CTO Fernanda Lima, Dir. Comercial Henrique Nobre
Nexatel LtdaCo-conspirator (leniency applicant)CEO Rodrigo Vaz
DataComm BrasilCo-conspiratorDir. André Santos
CADEBrazilian Competition AuthoritySuperintendência-Geral

18 Issue Tags — Definitions

#Tag (PT)EnglishDefinition
1Fixação de PreçosPrice FixingHorizontal agreement between competitors to fix, raise, or stabilize prices — Art. 36 §3 I Lei 12.529/2011
2Divisão de MercadoMarket AllocationAgreement to divide customers, territories, or products between competitors
3Fraude em LicitaçãoBid RiggingCoordinated manipulation of competitive bidding — especially on government tenders (pregões/licitações)
4Troca de InformaçõesInformation ExchangeSharing competitively sensitive information (prices, costs, customers) with competitors
5Coordenação de CartelCartel CoordinationGeneral cartel organization, meetings, and implementation of collusive agreements
6Abuso Posição DominanteMarket Dominance AbuseAbuse of dominant market position — Art. 36 §2 Lei 12.529/2011
7Fraude Contratos PúblicosGov. Contract FraudFraud on government procurement processes — Lei 8.666/93 + Lei 12.529
8Acordo de LeniênciaLeniency AgreementDocuments related to CADE leniency program negotiations — Art. 86 Lei 12.529
9Priv. Adv.-ClienteAttorney-Client PrivilegePrivileged communications between lawyer and client — Brazilian legal professional privilege (sigilo profissional)
10Produto do Trabalho JurídicoWork ProductAttorney work product — legal analysis, strategy memos prepared in anticipation of litigation
11Correspondência RegulatóriaRegulatory CorrespondenceCorrespondence with CADE, ANATEL, ANPD, or other Brazilian regulatory bodies
12Conf. de TerceirosThird-Party ConfidentialConfidential information belonging to third parties (clients, suppliers)
13Dados Pessoais LGPDPersonal Data (LGPD)Personal data subject to Lei Geral de Proteção de Dados (LGPD) — Brazil's data protection law
14Segredo ComercialTrade SecretProprietary business information — pricing models, customer data, technology roadmaps
15Proteção do WhistleblowerWhistleblower ProtectionDocuments related to internal or regulatory whistleblower disclosures
16Responsabilidade DiretoresDirector LiabilityPersonal liability of individual directors and officers under Lei 12.529 Art. 37
17Gun-Jumping (Pré-fusão)Gun-JumpingPremature implementation of merger before CADE approval — Art. 88 Lei 12.529
18Obstrução / Destruição ProvasObstructionDestruction or concealment of evidence — Art. 340 Código Penal + CADE sanctions
Brazilian Privilege Notes: Brazil recognizes attorney-client privilege (sigilo profissional) under OAB Statute Art. 7. In-house counsel (advogado empregado) communications may receive reduced privilege protection compared to outside counsel. CADE has broad investigative powers and can compel documents notwithstanding privilege claims — flag all potentially privileged documents for senior review.
Leniency Alert: Documents tagged Issue 8 (Leniency) must be treated with extreme care. Leniency negotiation communications are highly sensitive and may need separate privilege logging. Do not code these as 'produce' without senior attorney review.
Language Guidance: Key Portuguese terms — pregão = government e-auction; licitação = government tender; cartel = cartel (same word); reajuste = price adjustment; alinhamento = alignment (cartel term for coordination); leniência = leniency; processo administrativo = administrative proceeding.

🔒 Leadership Section

Restricted area for supervisors only — Jeff, Glory, Junior, Simon & Achumboro (Aaron). Use the sub-tabs below.

Tactical Meeting Reports

One report table per supervisor, grouped by the Person column on the Monday.com board.

Payments

Track payment records and status. Sensitive — supervisors only. Do not enter full bank details here.

Other Stuff

A flexible space for anything else leadership needs to track.

📌 Notes & Announcements

✅ Open Action Items

Carry forward unfinished tasks from meetings.

🕒 Timesheets

Track team hours by category. BH = Billable Hours, NBH = Non-Billable Hours. Edits save for all supervisors.

DateTeam MemberProject / MatterTaskBH
Total Billable0.0
DateTeam MemberActivityReason / NotesNBH
Total Non-Billable0.0

Examples of NBH: training, internal meetings, admin, downtime, QC calibration.
